one_line_takeaway: By repurposing guardrail models to score partial responses (the STAR signal) and using those scores to dynamically weight a CE-vs-KL finetuning loss, *DSS defends LLM finetuning against mixed-safety and evasion attacks that break binary example-level filtering, beating the strongest baseline by 20.41% without sacrificing task capability.
key_finding: In the worst-case scenario (malicious user uploading fully-harmful PureBad, provider has no trusted safe data), *DSS restores safety far better than all baselines: HEx-PHI 72.12% and AdvBench 89.42% safety, versus the strongest baseline RS at 56.36%/79.23% and collapsed vanilla SFT at 4.85%/3.27% — an improvement of 20.41% over RS, while retaining capability (MMLU 47.34%, ARC-C 59.31%, essentially matching SFT's 47.18%/58.71%). Robustness is the headline result: when a misleading suffix spikes the guardrail's false-negative rate from 3% to 34% (which breaks RS/SFT because they accept the full sequence), *DSS stays robust because earlier harmful chunks still receive low STAR scores and trigger KL regularization. Across guardrails, RS drops 22.88% under higher FN rates while *DSS declines only 3.84%. In the ideal case (benign user, safe data available), *DSS also leads on most benchmarks (HEx-PHI 86.06%, AdvBench 97.50%) and matches RS on GSM8K (35.10 vs 35.56).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'safety shaping' as a unifying frame for LLM finetuning-safety defenses and distinguishes static shaping (existing methods that make a binary keep-or-drop decision per training example) from the authors' proposed dynamic safety shaping (DSS), which re-weights the loss at the token level. Its three concrete contributions are: (1) an analysis showing that static shaping — exemplified by Rejection Sampling (RS), which discards guardrail-flagged samples — has exploitable blind spots because it treats each example atomically and cannot handle responses that turn unsafe mid-sequence; (2) the STAR score (Safety Trajectory Assessment of Response), a token-level safety signal produced by applying a guardrail model to PARTIALLY-completed responses to track how safety risk evolves across the response; and (3) *DSS (STAR-DSS), a new training loss that uses STAR scores to dynamically interpolate between cross-entropy imitation on safe segments and KL-regularization toward the reference model on unsafe segments, plus a theoretical bound on the finetuned model's harmfulness. Note: Deep Token [17, Qi et al.] is described as an 'early form of DSS' but is CITED prior work, not this paper's contribution — the authors position *DSS against it as a baseline.
threat_model: Setting is finetuning-as-a-service: a provider starts from a safety-aligned reference model (pi_ref), finetunes on user-uploaded data D_user (over which it has no control), and returns the updated model. The provider aims to preserve pi_ref's safety even though D_user may contain unsafe examples, uploaded deliberately (malicious user, high harmful proportion p up to 100%) or unintentionally (benign user, small p < 5%). The paper categorizes three finetuning risks: (1) vanilla harmful finetuning (harmful prompt-response pairs, or even benign data, dilute alignment); (2) prompt poisoning (harmful responses paired with backdoor trigger tokens, e.g. the trigger phrase 'Servius Astrumando Harmoniastra', so the model emits unsafe output when the trigger appears at inference); (3) response adaptation (harmful responses wrapped with safe-sounding prefixes PFX1 or misleading suffixes SFX1/SFX2 crafted to raise the perceived safety of the full sequence and evade guardrail filtering). It also evaluates a harmful-prefilling attack (an inference-time, not finetuning-time, threat where the user preloads a 'Sure, here is...' confirmation).
limitations: (1) Computational overhead: STAR requires extra guardrail forward passes over partial responses — Table 3 reports 1.15 min per 100 PureBad samples at M=1 with Granite Guardian-3.1-2B (2.77 min with the 8B guardrail), dropping to ~0.21-0.55 min at M=15; the authors argue this is a one-time precompute that can be cached and pipelined with training. (2) Smaller chunk size M gives finer granularity but higher cost, a granularity/cost tradeoff also present in the theoretical bound. (3) The method's safety is fundamentally tied to the guardrail's false-negative rate (the delta_chunk term), so it inherits the guardrail's blind spots, though token-level shaping mitigates full-sequence evasion. (4) The harmful-prefilling result is framed as a promising side benefit, but the authors explicitly note prefilling is an inference-time threat outside their finetuning-time defense scope. (5) Main case studies center on a small 1B model; larger-model results are relegated to the generalization figure/appendix.

datasets_used: Safe Instruct (source of trusted safe training samples D_safe)
quantitative_results: Worst-case RS baseline: HEx-PHI 56.36%, AdvBench 79.23%, MMLU 47.26%, ARC-C 58.88%
Worst-case vanilla SFT: HEx-PHI 4.85%, AdvBench 3.27% (safety collapse)
Ideal-case (Table 2) *DSS: HEx-PHI 86.06%, AdvBench 97.50%, MMLU 46.06%, ARC-C 58.33%, GSM8K 35.10% (matches RS's 35.56 on GSM8K)
Adversarial suffix raises Granite Guardian-3.1-2B FN rate from 3% to 34%
Llama Guard-3-8B has an 18% FN rate on PureBad; using it drops RS AdvBench safety from 79.23% (Granite Guardian) to 56.35%
SFX1 suffix drops AdvBench safety from 79.23% to 14.81% for guardrail-filtered training
Under higher-FN guardrails RS safety drops 22.88% vs *DSS only 3.84%
STAR compute cost (Table 3): 1.15 min/100 PureBad samples at M=1 (Granite Guardian-3.1-2B), 2.77 min with the 8B guardrail; ~0.21 min at M=15
Harm levels: with safe data included, *DSS reaches 90.19% AdvBench safety, surpassing the original off-the-shelf model
baselines_compared: Safe LoRA [22] (weight projection onto aligned-vs-pretrained direction)
Rejection Sampling / RS [53] (guardrail binary filtering; strongest static baseline)
Deep Token [17] (fixed KL penalty on first 5 tokens; 'early form of DSS')
Safe Instruct [14] (SFT on user+safe mixture; requires D_safe)
LISA [52] (alternates D_safe/D_user with l2 constraint; requires D_safe)
